meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 50 contains the Third Servant Song (vv. 4-9), where the servant describes his obedience and suffering: 'I gave my back to those who struck me.' A short but intense chapter of 11 verses in column XLI. The servant's willing submission to suffering is a key bridge to the Fourth Servant Song in chapters 52-53.
preamble.notable_variants: Verse 4: the scroll reads 'the tongue of those who are taught' identically. Verse 6: 'I gave my back to those who struck me' — identical in both traditions. Verse 9: a minor variant in the demonstrative pronoun. The Third Servant Song is remarkably stable across both traditions.
preamble.scroll_condition: Well preserved. Column XLI is fully legible.

variant_notes: No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
1QIsaiah-a uses a fuller form. Same meaning.

verses.variant_rendering: the tongue of those who are taught
verses.mt_rendering: the tongue of those who are taught
variant_notes: Identical consonantal reading. The servant declares that God gave him 'the tongue of those who are taught' (leshon limmudim) — the gift of prophetic speech. Both texts agree.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

Identical consonantal text. 'I gave my back to those who struck me, and my cheeks to those who pulled out the beard.' This verse, central to Christian interpretation of the Passion, is identical in the oldest scroll and the medieval MT.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

1QIsaiah-a reads hinneh ('behold,' fuller form) where the MT reads hen (shorter form). Both mean the same thing. 'Behold, the Lord GOD helps me; who will declare me guilty?' The servant's confidence in divine vindication is the same in both.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
